policy_statement: Create infiltration gardens and other aquifer recharge solutions (e.g., trenches and infiltration channels) as part of soil conservation and fertility improvement.
mechanism_description: Constructing infiltration-based recharge features increases groundwater replenishment, improving water supply reliability and drought resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (drought/variable rainfall) → ↑ groundwater recharge/storage → ↑ water-supply reliability → ↓ exposure/sensitivity to water scarcity
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Build and maintain infiltration-based recharge features that capture stormwater/runoff (or managed flows) and enhance percolation into aquifers.  
2) Mediating physical process: Increased infiltration and subsurface storage raises groundwater levels and recharge rates, smoothing temporal variability in water availability.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Improved reliability of water supply under drought and shifting rainfall patterns, reducing the likelihood and severity of water shortages.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increased drought and water-scarcity resilience by augmenting groundwater recharge and stored supply, reducing vulnerability to precipitation variability and drought.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Hydrologic buffering of drought/variability via increased groundwater recharge and storage leading to more reliable water supply.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Any GHG/energy effects (e.g., reduced pumping/treatment energy, avoided desalination) are context-dependent and not guaranteed; the mechanism’s direct, universal effect is reduced vulnerability to water scarcity.  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary: None—mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits may occur but are not unavoidable physical consequences across all implementations.  
4) Edge cases applied: If recharge uses energy-intensive pumping/treatment or induces additional water demand, net mitigation could be neutral/negative; this does not change the primary adaptation function.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: All aquifer recharge infrastructure aims to increase groundwater replenishment and thereby improve drought/water-supply resilience, fitting Adaptation universally.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations may deliver Resource Efficiency (reduced reliance on imported/desalinated water) or Mitigation co-benefits (lower energy for water supply) but these depend on the baseline water-source mix and operational design; conversely, energy-intensive managed recharge can increase emissions—hence no inherent secondary category.
additional_secondary_evidence: Water security: Portugal faces drought and water scarcity, and creating infiltration gardens/trenches/channels directly reduces exposure/sensitivity because it increases groundwater recharge and subsurface storage, improving reliability of water supply during dry periods and variable rainfall.
key_indicators: “Creation of infiltration gardens”; “aquifer recharge solutions”; “trenches”; “infiltration channels”
co_benefits: Potential flood mitigation (stormwater retention/infiltration) and soil conservation/fertility improvement, but not explicitly linked to reducing flood exposure in the text.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Infiltration gardens” can also function as urban stormwater management (flood reduction), but the verbatim text emphasizes aquifer recharge rather than flood-risk reduction, so no additional flood-related secondary is assigned.
